syllabus.topic: Course Context & Motivation for SVMs in Pattern Representation Learning
sub_points: Position of SVMs within pattern recognition and representation learning workflows
Why margin-based classification is useful for generalization
High-level overview of what an SVM optimizes (separating hyperplane with maximum margin)

syllabus.topic: Recap: Linear Classifiers as a Starting Point
sub_points: Linear decision functions (hyperplanes) and geometric interpretation
Classification rule, decision boundary, and role of weights/bias
Motivation for moving beyond generic linear classifiers to maximum-margin formulations

syllabus.topic: Hard-Margin SVM: Maximum-Margin Classification for Separable Data
sub_points: Hard-margin constraints and geometric margin definition
Primal optimization formulation and conversion to a Quadratic Program (QP)
Lagrangian and KKT conditions; deriving the dual problem from the primal
Interpretation of support vectors and how the decision boundary is determined from them
Decision function construction from dual variables; conceptual checks/questions

syllabus.topic: Non-separable Data & Soft-Margin SVM (Slack Variables and Regularization)
sub_points: Why real data are often not linearly separable and what breaks in hard-margin SVM
Slack variables to allow margin violations; hinge-loss-like behavior
Soft-margin primal objective with regularization parameter (trade-off between margin and violations)
Soft-margin dual derivation and implications (boxed constraints on dual variables)
Comparing hard vs soft margin: robustness to noise/outliers and effect on decision boundary

syllabus.topic: Kernel SVM: Nonlinear Decision Boundaries via the Kernel Trick
sub_points: Feature-space viewpoint: linear SVM in transformed space induces nonlinear boundaries in input space
Kernel trick in the dual: replacing inner products with kernel functions without explicit mapping
Common kernels (e.g., polynomial, RBF/Gaussian) and typical use cases
Kernel selection considerations and hyperparameter effects (capacity, smoothness, overfitting risk)
Validity of kernels: conditions for a function to be a kernel (positive semidefinite / Mercer condition intuition)
Extending binary SVMs to multi-class classification
One-vs-rest and one-vs-one decomposition strategies and their training/inference implications
Practical considerations: number of classifiers, computational cost, and decision aggregation
Pointers to additional resources for theoretical foundations and practical implementations
Suggested references for kernels, convex optimization, and SVM generalization theory
Materials for deeper study and problem practice
